sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A physics lab launched identical 0.20 kg balls from a spring launcher at a speed of 20 m/s while varying launch angle. Air resistance was treated as negligible and g = 10 m/s². Students measured horizontal range and maximum height. Approximate ranges for 20°, 30°, 45°, 60°, and 70° were 26 m, 35 m, 40 m, 35 m, and 26 m. Maximum heights were about 2.3 m, 5.0 m, 10 m, 15 m, and 17.7 m. The apparatus launched each ball from the same height as the landing surface.
questions.stem: Which launch angle produced the greatest horizontal range in Figure 1?

explanations.A: Correct. For a projectile launched and landing at the same height with fixed speed, maximum range occurs at 45°. The figure also shows the largest range, 40 m, at 45°.
explanations.B: The 20° launch produced a much shorter range of about 26 m.
explanations.C: The 60° launch has the same range as 30° and is below the 45° range.
explanations.D: The 70° launch has high vertical motion but a shorter horizontal range.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Horizontal range and maximum height for projectiles launched at 20 m/s from level ground.

questions.stem: Why do the 30° and 60° launches have nearly equal ranges?

choices.B: They have complementary angles with the same value of sin(2θ)
choices.A: They have the same vertical velocity component
choices.C: They have the same time of flight

explanations.B: Correct. For level-ground projectile motion, R = v²sin(2θ)/g. Since sin(60°) = sin(120°), 30° and 60° are complementary launch angles with the same range for a fixed speed.
explanations.A: The 60° launch has a larger vertical component than the 30° launch.
explanations.C: The 60° launch stays in the air longer, so time of flight is not the same.
explanations.D: The maximum heights differ substantially because vertical velocity differs.

questions.stem: What is the approximate maximum height for the 45° launch?

explanations.C: Correct. At 45°, the vertical component is 20sin45° ≈ 14.1 m/s. Maximum height is vy²/(2g) ≈ 200/20 = 10 m, matching the Figure 1 value for the 45° launch.
explanations.A: 20 m is too high; it would require roughly twice the vertical kinetic energy reported for the 45° launch.
explanations.B: 40 m is the horizontal range at 45°, not the maximum height.
explanations.D: 5 m is close to the 30° maximum height, not the 45° maximum height.

questions.stem: If air resistance were significant, what would happen to the measured range relative to the ideal prediction?

choices.C: It would become independent of angle

explanations.D: Correct. Air resistance does negative work on the projectile and reduces horizontal speed during flight. Therefore the actual range would be smaller than the ideal no-drag prediction.
explanations.A: Drag dissipates mechanical energy, so it does not increase range.
explanations.B: Air resistance is an external force and changes the trajectory.
explanations.C: Angle would still affect the launch components and the trajectory.

questions.stem: Which assumption is required for using R = v²sin(2θ)/g?

choices.A: The projectile lands at the same vertical height from which it is launched

explanations.A: Correct. The simple range equation assumes level-ground motion, constant downward acceleration g, and no air resistance. If launch and landing heights differ, the time of flight changes and the formula no longer applies directly.
explanations.B: Projectile range equations do not require zero mass; mass cancels when drag is neglected.
explanations.C: A 90° launch produces no horizontal range.
explanations.D: Vertical acceleration is g downward, not zero.

questions.passage: A biomechanics demonstration models the forearm as a rigid bar held horizontally. A 40 N weight is placed 0.30 m from the elbow joint. The biceps inserts 0.040 m from the elbow and pulls approximately perpendicular to the forearm. Students ignore the forearm’s own weight for the first calculation, then repeat the model with an added 15 N forearm weight acting 0.15 m from the elbow. The elbow joint supplies whatever force is needed for translational equilibrium.
questions.stem: Ignoring forearm weight, what biceps force is needed to balance the 40 N hand weight?

explanations.B: Correct. The external torque is 40 N × 0.30 m = 12 N·m. With a 0.040 m biceps lever arm, equilibrium requires Fbiceps = 12/0.040 = 300 N.
explanations.A: 30 N underestimates the force by a factor of 10.
explanations.C: 120 N would correspond to using a 0.10 m lever arm, not 0.040 m.
explanations.D: 12 N is the torque in N·m, not the biceps force.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. External torque from a hand-held weight, forearm-weight torque, and biceps lever arm in the forearm model.

questions.stem: What is the torque due to the forearm’s own weight when it is included?

explanations.C: Correct. Torque equals force times perpendicular distance. The forearm contributes 15 N × 0.15 m = 2.25 N·m about the elbow.
explanations.A: 15 N is the force, not the torque.
explanations.B: 0.60 N·m would use the biceps insertion distance, not the forearm center of mass.
explanations.D: 6.0 N·m would require a different force-distance product.

questions.stem: Including the forearm’s weight changes the required biceps force to approximately what value?

explanations.D: Correct. The total flexion-opposing torque is 12 + 2.25 = 14.25 N·m. Dividing by the 0.040 m biceps lever arm gives about 356 N.
explanations.A: 244 N results from subtracting the forearm torque even though it acts in the same direction as the hand weight.
explanations.B: 300 N ignores the added forearm-weight torque.
explanations.C: 56 N is the additional force from forearm weight alone, not the total required force.

questions.stem: Why is the biceps force so much larger than the hand weight?

choices.A: The biceps acts with a much shorter lever arm than the hand weight
choices.B: Muscle forces are not subject to Newton’s laws
choices.C: The hand weight is massless in rotational problems
choices.D: The elbow force must always be zero

explanations.A: Correct. Torque balance requires force times lever arm to match on both sides. Because the biceps inserts very close to the elbow, it must exert a large force to balance the torque from a smaller weight farther from the pivot.
explanations.B: Muscle forces still obey Newtonian mechanics.
explanations.C: The hand weight clearly exerts a 40 N force and produces torque.
explanations.D: The elbow joint force may be nonzero; translational and rotational equilibrium are separate conditions.

questions.stem: If the weight were moved farther from the elbow while force stayed 40 N, what would happen to required biceps force?

explanations.B: Correct. Moving the weight farther increases its lever arm and therefore its torque. To maintain rotational equilibrium, the biceps must exert a larger force through its fixed short lever arm.
explanations.A: A larger external torque cannot be balanced by a smaller biceps force with the same lever arm.
explanations.C: Torque depends on distance from the pivot, so the force requirement changes.
explanations.D: The biceps force would not become zero when external torque increases.
